quote: So we're actually a a huge company. We are nationwide, and so we're we're we're all over. But, yeah, we have our Where is headquarters? Chicago.
stewart_read: Holland appeared to hesitate mid-sentence when Kevin asked about headquarters — the transcript shows 'we're we're we're all over' followed by what reads as a partial or uncertain answer before Kevin's follow-up question prompted the correction to Utah. Any visible uncertainty on a basic company fact registers with a skeptical buyer who is actively vetting the company. Holland recovered quickly, but the stumble came on the first of Kevin's three legitimacy probes.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Holland was simply mid-thought and the transcript captures a natural pause, not a factual error. Rejected — the stutter pattern ('we're we're we're') and the sequence of Kevin needing to ask again ('Where is headquarters?') suggest the uncertainty was perceptible to Kevin.
coaching_implication: Drill the legitimacy anchor. Holland should be able to deliver company HQ, founding story, and panel origin in one clean 15-second sequence without hesitation. Kevin was vetting — every stumble on a basic fact costs trust with this buyer profile. Role-play the company-vetting sequence until it's automatic.

quote: I do want to clarify. Is your roof it's just a regular asphalt shingle roof, it looks like. Yes. Do you do you know how old that is?
stewart_read: Kevin's roof is 15-20 years old — right at the schema's hard-DQ threshold (roof_age_over_15_no_replacement_planned). Holland captured the data and responded 'Yeah. Okay. Yeah. Sounds good.' with no follow-up on replacement plans and no bridge to Ion's in-house roofing offering. This is a dual miss: a potential DQ risk that wasn't resolved, and a cross-sell signal that wasn't routed.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Holland noted the roof age and planned to surface it at the closer stage. Rejected — the setter's job is to flag DQ risks and route cross-sell signals at the setter stage. Deferring a potential hard-DQ to the closer wastes the closer's time and risks a no-sit.
coaching_implication: When a customer reports a 15+ year roof, the setter has two required moves: (1) ask whether a replacement is planned or being considered, and (2) if yes, bridge to Ion's roofing team per cross_sell_signals.roof_replacement_planned. Holland did neither. Show her the SESSION18_fcbf33c1 example of the 4-second roofing bridge. The question to drill: 'Have you thought about replacing the roof, or is that something you'd want to look at?' — it opens both the DQ resolution and the cross-sell path.

quote: Well, not all not only the best deal, but there's a sustainability deal. You know what I mean? And reliability. You know what I mean? Ability. Yep. Right. Reliability and then, k. And most importantly, the ability to pay.
stewart_read: Kevin volunteered a three-part values hierarchy — sustainability, reliability, ability to pay — unprompted. This is the richest emotional anchor of the call. The schema identifies verify.interest_in_solar as the moment where 'depth matters more than the answer' and where top setters spend 60+ seconds. Holland responded to each value with a single-word acknowledgment ('Yeah,' 'Yep,' 'Yeah. K.') and moved on. The bill-swap framing introduced earlier at 03:02 would have landed significantly harder if it had been tied back to Kevin's 'ability to pay' framing here.
coaching_implication: This is the call's highest-leverage missed anchor. Manager should replay this sequence and ask Holland: 'What could you have said here?' The suggested move is to reflect the values back and tie them to the appointment: something like 'You just named the three things that matter most — sustainability, reliability, and whether it actually saves you money. That's exactly what the specialist walks through with the real numbers for your home. That's the conversation worth having.' No fabricated numbers needed — Kevin's own framing is the leverage.

quote: So I will be honest with you. I'm not exactly sure, like, what the cost of your system would be, but I can say that we have a we don't do any money upfront or out of pocket.
stewart_read: Holland's bill-swap pivot is directionally correct — she redirected a pricing question to the bill-replacement concept rather than fabricating a number. The honesty ('I'm not exactly sure') is appropriate. However, the pivot floated without grounding because Kevin's bill had not yet been established. The bill-swap framing ('replace your current bill with a cheaper solar bill') has no emotional weight when the listener's actual bill pain is unknown. This is a sequencing gap, not a protocol violation.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Holland introduced the bill-swap concept early to create curiosity before collecting the bill, planning to anchor it concretely once the bill was in hand. Partially supported — but the bill was never collected, so the anchor was never completed.
coaching_implication: The bill-swap concept is the right move; the timing undercut it. Coach Holland on the capture-then-flip sequence from coaching_philosophy.bill_anchoring: establish the bill first, then use the customer's actual number as the emotional anchor for the swap framing. The question to drill: 'What are you paying ComEd right now?' before introducing the bill-swap concept.

quote: Alright. K. And then, yeah, right now so we have a pretty decent program. What it does is it guarantees you savings within the first year you have solar. As long as you have a credit score above 6 70, then you'll qualify.
stewart_read: Holland delivered the credit qualifier using language close to the schema template ('guarantees you savings within the first year'). The credit threshold of 670 matches the schema. The sequencing issue is that this qualifier was delivered before the bill had been collected — the 'guaranteed savings' framing has no grounding without the customer's actual usage data. This is a sequencing note, not a script violation. Kevin's response ('I am far above that') confirms he passed the qualifier.
coaching_implication: The qualifier language is script-compliant. The coaching note is sequencing: the savings guarantee lands harder when it follows bill collection, because the specialist can then tie the guarantee to the customer's actual number. Holland delivered the qualifier into a vacuum. Reinforce the call-shape beat order from call_shape.required_beats — bill_collection (beat 4) should precede the qualifier framing.

quote: I'm kind of indisposed post right now with with a grandchild, and now is not a real good time to do it. Can you text me, the the information?
stewart_read: Kevin's exit is situational — he's with a grandchild and can't pull up the bill right now. This is not a hard objection; it's a timing constraint. Holland accepted the text deferral without attempting to lock a callback time, set a specific follow-up window, or complete any element of the button_up protocol. The call ended with zero of the six required button_up elements completed. The text loop is open but unanchored.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Holland recognized that pushing for a locked time while Kevin was distracted with a grandchild would create friction, and chose to preserve goodwill by accepting the deferral. Partially supported by Kevin's warm parting tone — but the strategic read requires that Holland had a plan to lock the time in the text, which the transcript does not confirm.
coaching_implication: Even when a customer can't complete the bill collection live, the setter can still lock a callback time before hanging up: 'No problem at all — when's a good time tomorrow for me to follow up with you?' That one question converts an open text loop into a scheduled callback. Holland should drill this pivot. The text itself should include a specific proposed time per text_after_call protocol.

quote: And then I have you down as the property owner. Is there anything else you want? Along along along with my wife.
stewart_read: Holland surfaced the wife on title — Kevin confirmed it. Holland's response was 'Okay. Along with your wife. K. Awesome.' and moved on. The schema is explicit: co-owner on title means the spouse MUST be included in the appointment. Holland captured the fact but did not take the required action of building the appointment around both Kevin and his wife's availability. Since no appointment was ultimately booked, this didn't produce a structural vulnerability in a confirmed sit — but the pattern of capturing the spouse fact and not acting on it is worth flagging.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Holland noted the wife on title and planned to include her when scheduling the appointment. Partially supported — but no appointment was booked, and Holland never referenced the wife again in the call.
coaching_implication: The moment Holland hears 'along with my wife,' the next question should be: 'Perfect — we'll want her on the call too since she's on the title. What time works for both of you?' This is protocols.spouse_decision — it's a procedure, not a persuasion move. Drill the reflex: co-owner confirmation → immediate availability question for both.

quote: Hey. Thank you. And where did you located at?
stewart_read: Kevin initiated a personal question after the call was effectively over — asking where Holland is located, then engaging on Utah weather and water concerns. This is a residual engagement signal: Kevin is still curious about the person he spoke with, which means his interest in the conversation hasn't fully cooled. This mitigates the 'high probability of going cold' read on the text-loop outcome. A well-crafted follow-up text that references Kevin's stated values (sustainability, reliability, ability to pay) and his family solar context has a real chance of re-engaging him.
coaching_implication: Surface this moment for Holland as evidence that Kevin liked her. The follow-up text is not a formality — it's the continuation of a conversation Kevin was still in when the call ended. The text should reference something specific Kevin said (his children's solar systems, his 'ability to pay' framing, or the grandchild moment) per text_after_call.required_elements. Generic 'looking forward to chatting' texts will not re-engage this buyer.
